Convert the annual effective rate to a constant force of interest: \(\delta=\ln(1+i)=\ln(1.0710715)=0.06866\).
generalization: For a constant force of interest \(\delta\), accumulation is given by \(e^{\delta t}\) and discounting is given by \(e^{-\delta t}\). If the present value and future value are known, solve \(PV=FVe^{-\delta t}\) for \(\delta\) using logarithms.
answer_text: The constant force of interest is \(\boxed{0.06866}\), so the correct choice is C.

topic: unknown rate of interest and equations of value
title: Exercise 77
statement: David can receive one of the following two payment streams: (i) \(100\) at time \(0\), \(200\) at time \(n\), and \(300\) at time \(2n\); (ii) \(600\) at time \(10\). At an annual effective interest rate of \(i\), the present values of the two streams are equal. Given \(v^n=0.75941\), determine \(i\).
solution_steps: Choose time \(0\) as the comparison date.
The present value of the first stream is \(100+200v^n+300v^{2n}\).
The present value of the second stream is \(600v^{10}\).
Since the present values are equal, \(600v^{10}=100+200v^n+300v^{2n}\).
generalization: When an equation of value contains powers such as \(v^n\) and \(v^{2n}\), use the given value of \(v^n\) to simplify the equation before solving for the rate.
answer_text: The annual effective interest rate is \(\boxed{0.03511}\), so the correct choice is C.

topic: unknown rate of interest and equations of value
title: Exercise 78
statement: Mike receives cash flows of \(100\) today, \(200\) in one year, and \(100\) in two years. The present value of these cash flows is \(364.46\) at an annual effective rate of interest \(i\). Calculate \(i\).
solution_steps: Choose time \(0\) as the comparison date.
This gives \(v=0.909085\) or a negative value.
Alternatively, using the cash flow function, subtract the time-0 cash flow from the present value: \(364.46-100=264.46\).
Using one valid sign convention, enter \(CF_0=-264.46\), \(C01=200\), \(F01=1\), \(C02=100\), and \(F02=1\).
generalization: When the unknown is the interest rate, write an equation of value in terms of the discount factor \(v=\dfrac{1}{1+i}\). If the equation is quadratic in \(v\), solve using the quadratic formula and reject any negative discount factor.
answer_text: The annual effective interest rate is \(\boxed{0.10}\), so the correct choice is C.

topic: unknown rate of interest and outstanding balance
title: Exercise 79
statement: John borrows \(1{,}000\) from Jane at an annual effective rate of interest \(i\). He agrees to pay back \(1{,}000\) after six years and \(1{,}366.87\) after another six years. Three years after his first payment, John repays the outstanding balance. What is the amount of John's second payment?
solution_steps: Under the original repayment plan, John pays \(1{,}000\) at time \(6\) and \(1{,}366.87\) at time \(12\).
Use the original repayment plan to find the interest rate.
Using the quadratic formula gives a positive solution \(x=0.56447\).
John makes his first payment at time \(6\), then repays the outstanding balance three years later, at time \(9\).
At time \(9\), the only remaining scheduled payment is \(1{,}366.87\) due at time \(12\).
Therefore, the outstanding balance at time \(9\) is \(X=1{,}366.87v^3\).
Therefore, John's second payment is approximately \(1{,}027\).
generalization: To find an outstanding balance at a given time, take the value at that time of the remaining scheduled payments. If the interest rate is not given directly, first use the original equation of value to solve for the rate.
answer_text: John's second payment is approximately \(\boxed{1{,}027}\), so the correct choice is B.

topic: unknown rate of interest and equations of value
title: Exercise 80
statement: At an annual effective interest rate \(i\), with \(i>0\), the following are all equal: (i) the present value of \(10{,}000\) at the end of \(6\) years; (ii) the sum of the present values of \(6{,}000\) at the end of year \(t\) and \(56{,}000\) at the end of year \(2t\); (iii) \(5{,}000\) immediately. Calculate the present value of a payment of \(8{,}000\) at the end of year \(t+3\), using the same annual effective interest rate.
solution_steps: Since the present value of \(10{,}000\) at the end of \(6\) years is equal to \(5{,}000\) immediately, we have \(10{,}000v^6=5{,}000\).
Now use the equality between the second payment stream and \(5{,}000\) immediately: \(5{,}000=6{,}000v^t+56{,}000v^{2t}\).
This gives \(x=\dfrac{-6+34}{112}=\dfrac{28}{112}=\dfrac{1}{4}\), discarding the negative solution.
The present value of \(8{,}000\) at time \(t+3\) is \(8{,}000v^{t+3}\).
generalization: When several present values are known to be equal, use the simplest equality first to obtain information about the discount factor. Then use another equality to solve for expressions such as \(v^t\), without necessarily needing to solve directly for \(t\).
answer_text: The present value is approximately \(\boxed{1{,}415}\), so the correct choice is B.

topic: unknown rate of interest and force of interest
title: Exercise 81
statement: At time \(t=0\), Donald puts \(1{,}000\) into a fund crediting interest at a nominal annual rate of \(i\), compounded semiannually. At time \(t=2\), Lewis puts \(1{,}000\) into a different fund crediting interest at a force of interest \(\delta_t=\dfrac{1}{5+t}\), for all \(t\). At time \(t=16\), the amounts in each fund will be equal. Calculate \(i\).
solution_steps: For Lewis, identify the accumulation function from the force of interest.
Donald accumulates from time \(0\) to time \(16\). Since his nominal annual rate is \(i\), compounded semiannually, the effective semiannual rate is \(\dfrac{i}{2}\).
Therefore, Donald's accumulated value at time \(16\) is \(1{,}000\left(1+\dfrac{i}{2}\right)^{32}\).
Lewis accumulates from time \(2\) to time \(16\). His accumulation factor is \(\dfrac{a(16)}{a(2)}\).
Since the two funds are equal at time \(16\), set \(1{,}000\left(1+\dfrac{i}{2}\right)^{32}=1{,}000\cdot 3\).
generalization: When comparing two funds at a common future date, write the accumulated value of each fund at that date. For a force of interest, either integrate the force over the relevant interval or identify the accumulation function and use the ratio \(\dfrac{a(t_2)}{a(t_1)}\).
answer_text: The nominal annual rate compounded semiannually is approximately \(\boxed{7.0\%}\), so the correct choice is B.

title: Exercise 82
statement: A payment of \(1\) is to be made each year for \(22\) years. The effective annual rate of interest is \(8.25\%\). If the first payment is one year from now, the present value of these payments is \(10\). Calculate the present value if the first payment is \(22\) years from now.
solution_steps: If the first payment is one year from now, then the payments occur at times \(1,2,\ldots,22\).
We are given that the present value of this annuity-immediate is \(\annimm{22}=10\).
If the first payment is \(22\) years from now, then the payments occur at times \(22,23,\ldots,43\).
This is still a 22-payment annuity-immediate, but \(\annimm{22}\) is measured one period before the first payment.
Since the first payment is at time \(22\), the value \(\annimm{22}=10\) is measured at time \(21\).
To find the present value at time \(0\), discount this value by \(21\) years.
generalization: The value \(\annimm{n}\) is measured one period before the first payment. If the first payment is delayed, first locate the time at which the annuity value is measured, then discount that value back to the present.
answer_text: The present value is \(\boxed{1.89}\), so the correct choice is C.

title: Exercise 83
statement: John invests \(100{,}000\) into a fund earning an annual effective interest rate of \(4\%\). John will make withdrawals of \(X\) at the end of every \(5\) years for a total of \(10\) withdrawals. After the last withdrawal, the fund's balance will be \(0\). Calculate \(X\).
solution_steps: The withdrawals are made every \(5\) years, so we use the effective interest rate per \(5\)-year period.
Let \(j\) be the effective rate per \(5\) years.
Since the annual effective interest rate is \(4\%\), we have \(1+j=(1.04)^5\).
The withdrawals form an annuity-immediate with \(10\) payments of \(X\), where each payment period is \(5\) years.
Therefore, the present value of the withdrawals is \(X\annimm{10}_{j}\).
The equation of value at time \(0\) is \(100{,}000=X\annimm{10}_{j}\).
generalization: When annuity payments are made every \(k\) years, convert the annual effective interest rate into an effective rate per \(k\)-year payment period before using the annuity formula.
answer_text: The withdrawal amount is \(\boxed{25{,}213.09}\), so the correct choice is C.

title: Exercise 84
statement: A 20-year annuity pays \(100\) every other year beginning at the end of the second year, with additional payments of \(300\) each at the ends of years \(3\), \(9\), and \(15\). The effective annual interest rate is \(4\%\). Calculate the present value of the annuity.
solution_steps: The regular payments of \(100\) occur every other year beginning at time \(2\), so they occur at times \(2,4,6,\ldots,20\).
Since the payment period is \(2\) years, use the effective interest rate per \(2\)-year period.
Let \(j\) be the effective interest rate per \(2\) years. Then \(1+j=(1.04)^2\).
The present value of the regular payments is \(100\annimm{10}_{j}\).
The additional payments of \(300\) occur at times \(3\), \(9\), and \(15\).
So the present value is approximately \(1{,}310\).
generalization: When payments occur every \(k\) years, convert the annual effective interest rate into an effective rate per \(k\)-year payment period for the annuity formula. Payments that do not fit the regular annuity pattern should be valued separately and added.
answer_text: The present value is approximately \(\boxed{1{,}310}\), so the correct choice is A.

title: Exercise 85
statement: On January 1, 1985, Marc has the following two options for repaying a loan: (i) sixty monthly payments of \(100\), commencing February 1, 1985; (ii) a single payment of \(6{,}000\) at the end of \(K\) months. Interest is at a nominal annual rate of \(12\%\), compounded monthly. The two options have the same present value. Determine \(K\).
solution_steps: The nominal annual interest rate is \(12\%\), compounded monthly.
Therefore, the effective monthly interest rate is \(j=\dfrac{0.12}{12}=0.01\).
The first option consists of \(60\) monthly payments of \(100\), beginning one month from January 1, 1985.
The second option is a single payment of \(6{,}000\) at the end of \(K\) months, so its present value is \(6{,}000v_{0.01}^{K}\).
Since the two options have the same present value, \(100\annimm{60}_{0.01}=6{,}000v_{0.01}^{K}\).
generalization: When payments are monthly and the interest rate is nominal convertible monthly, use the monthly effective rate directly. A single payment at the end of \(K\) months has present value \(Cv^K\), while a level monthly annuity-immediate has present value \(R\annimm{n}\).
answer_text: The value of \(K\) is approximately \(\boxed{29.0}\), so the correct choice is A.

topic: annuities-immediate and unknown rate of interest
title: Exercise 86
statement: An annuity pays \(2\) at the end of each year for \(18\) years. Another annuity pays \(2.5\) at the end of each year for \(9\) years. At an effective annual interest rate of \(i\), where \(0<i<1\), the present values of both annuities are equal. Calculate \(i\).
solution_steps: The present value of the first annuity is \(2\annimm{18}\).
The present value of the second annuity is \(2.5\annimm{9}\).
Since the present values are equal, \(2\annimm{18}=2.5\annimm{9}\).
Using the quadratic formula gives \(x=1\) or \(x=0.25\).
The solution \(v^9=1\) implies \(v=1\), hence \(i=0\), which is not allowed because \(0<i<1\).
generalization: When two annuity present values are equal, write both values using \(\annimm{n}=\dfrac{1-v^n}{i}\). If the terms involve powers like \(v^9\) and \(v^{18}\), use a substitution such as \(x=v^9\) to reduce the equation to a quadratic.
answer_text: The effective annual interest rate is approximately \(\boxed{16.653\%}\), so the correct choice is B.

title: Exercise 87
statement: A fifteen-year annuity-immediate has payments of \(500\) per year. The rate of interest is \(3.5\%\) for the first nine years and \(5\%\) thereafter. What is the present value of this annuity?
solution_steps: The first \(9\) payments occur at times \(1,2,\ldots,9\).
These payments are valued using the interest rate \(3.5\%\).
The remaining \(6\) payments occur at times \(10,11,\ldots,15\).
These payments are valued at time \(9\) using the interest rate \(5\%\), then discounted back to time \(0\) using the rate \(3.5\%\).
The present value of the first \(9\) payments is \(500\annimm{9}_{0.035}\).
The value at time \(9\) of the last \(6\) payments is \(500\annimm{6}_{0.05}\).
Discount this value back to time \(0\) using \(v_{0.035}^{9}\).
generalization: When interest rates change over time, split the annuity into blocks. Value each block using the interest rate that applies during that block, then move each block value to the comparison date using the appropriate discount factors.
answer_text: The present value is \(\boxed{5{,}665.94}\), so the correct choice is C.

title: Exercise 88
statement: John invests \(100{,}000\) into a fund earning an annual effective interest rate of \(4\%\). John will make withdrawals of \(X\) at the end of every \(5\) years for a total of \(10\) withdrawals. After the last withdrawal, the fund's balance will be \(0\). Calculate \(X\).
solution_steps: The withdrawals are made every \(5\) years, so we use the effective interest rate per \(5\)-year period.
Let \(j\) be the effective rate per \(5\) years.
Since the annual effective interest rate is \(4\%\), we have \(1+j=(1.04)^5\).
generalization: When annuity payments are made every \(k\) years, the TVM interest rate must be the effective rate per \(k\)-year payment period. The calculator input \(I/Y\) is usually entered as a percentage, so use \(100j\), not \(j\), when entering the rate.
answer_text: The withdrawal amount is \(\boxed{25{,}213.09}\), so the correct choice is C.

title: Exercise 89
statement: Payments are made every \(5\) years, with the first payment on July 1, 2014 and the last payment on July 1, 2064. Determine the number of payments.
solution_steps: The payments are made every \(5\) years.
The first payment is in \(2014\), and the last payment is in \(2064\).
Using the previous term method, the term before \(2014\) is \(2014-5=2009\).
Therefore, the number of payments is \(\dfrac{2064-2009}{5}\).
generalization: For payments made at regular intervals, the number of payments is \(\dfrac{\text{last payment time}-\text{first payment time}}{\text{spacing}}+1\). Equivalently, subtract the previous term from the last payment time and divide by the spacing.
answer_text: There are \(\boxed{11}\) payments, so the correct choice is C.

title: Exercise 90
statement: John has a \(30\)-year \(100{,}000\) mortgage with monthly payments based on a nominal annual interest rate of \(12\%\), convertible monthly. The first payment is due in one month. Find \(X\) such that if John decided to add \(X\) to each monthly payment, starting with the first payment, the term of the mortgage would be reduced to \(25\) years.
solution_steps: The nominal annual interest rate is \(12\%\), convertible monthly.
Therefore, the effective monthly interest rate is \(j=\dfrac{0.12}{12}=0.01\).
Let \(R\) be the original monthly payment for the \(30\)-year mortgage.
If John reduces the term to \(25\) years, there are \(25\cdot 12=300\) monthly payments.
Thus, \(X\) is at least \(20\), but less than \(30\).
generalization: For a level-payment mortgage with monthly payments, use the monthly effective interest rate and treat the payments as an annuity-immediate. To compare two mortgage terms, compute the monthly payment for each term and take the difference.
answer_text: The additional monthly payment is \(\boxed{24.61}\), so the correct choice is B.

topic: annuities-immediate and unknown rate of interest
title: Exercise 91
statement: Eric receives \(12{,}000\) from a life insurance policy. He uses the fund to purchase two different annuities, each costing \(6{,}000\). The first annuity is a \(24\)-year annuity-immediate paying \(K\) per year to himself. The second annuity is an \(8\)-year annuity-immediate paying \(2K\) per year to his son. Both annuities are based on an annual effective interest rate of \(i\), where \(i>0\). Determine \(i\).
solution_steps: Rewrite the \(24\)-year annuity as three deferred \(8\)-year annuities: \(\annimm{24}_{i}=\annimm{8}_{i}+v^8\annimm{8}_{i}+v^{16}\annimm{8}_{i}\).
generalization: A long annuity can often be split into smaller deferred annuities. For example, \(\annimm{24}=\annimm{8}+v^8\annimm{8}+v^{16}\annimm{8}\). This can turn an unknown-rate annuity equation into a polynomial equation in \(v^n\).
answer_text: The annual effective interest rate is approximately \(\boxed{6.2\%}\), so the correct choice is B.

title: Exercise 92
statement: Grandma decides to set up a college fund for her newborn grandson. She will deposit \(1{,}335.10\) into a savings account every year on his birthday, starting on his first birthday and ending on his \(18\)th birthday. She hopes to accumulate \(50{,}000\) to give to him on his \(18\)th birthday. What is the minimum annual compound interest rate the savings account must earn in order to reach her goal?
solution_steps: The deposits are made on birthdays \(1,2,\ldots,18\).
Since the goal is to have \(50{,}000\) on the \(18\)th birthday, we need the accumulated value at time \(18\).
The deposits form an annuity-immediate with \(18\) payments of \(1{,}335.10\).
Solving this equation with a financial calculator gives \(I/Y=8\).
Thus, the account must earn at least \(8\%\).
generalization: When equal deposits are made at the end of each period and the target amount is at the time of the last deposit, use the accumulated value annuity-immediate formula \(Rs_{\overline{n}\,|i}\). If the interest rate is unknown, solve the resulting equation for \(i\), often using a financial calculator or numerical solver.
answer_text: The minimum annual compound interest rate is \(\boxed{8\%}\), so the correct choice is C.

topic: annuities-immediate and accumulated value with force of interest
title: Exercise 93
statement: You are given \(\delta_t=\dfrac{1}{1+t}\), for \(0\leq t\leq 5\). Calculate \(s_{\overline{5}\,|}\).
solution_steps: First identify the accumulation function from the force of interest.
The symbol \(s_{\overline{5}\,|}\) represents the accumulated value at time \(5\) of five payments of \(1\), made at times \(1,2,3,4,5\).
A payment made at time \(k\) accumulates to time \(5\) by the factor \(\dfrac{a(5)}{a(k)}\).
generalization: When the force of interest is varying, do not use the constant-rate formula \(s_{\overline{n}\,|}=\dfrac{(1+i)^n-1}{i}\). Instead, find the accumulation function \(a(t)\), then accumulate each payment from its payment time to the valuation time using \(\dfrac{a(T)}{a(t)}\).
answer_text: The accumulated value is \(\boxed{8.7}\), so the correct choice is C.

title: Exercise 94
statement: A person age \(40\) wishes to accumulate a fund for retirement by depositing an amount \(X\) at the end of each year into an account paying \(4\%\) interest. At age \(65\), the person will use the entire account balance to purchase a 15-year annuity-immediate with annual payments of \(10{,}000\), based on an annual effective interest rate of \(5\%\). Find \(X\).
solution_steps: The person deposits \(X\) at the end of each year from age \(41\) through age \(65\).
The accumulated value of these deposits at age \(65\) is \(Xs_{\overline{25}\,|0.04}\).
At age \(65\), the person purchases a 15-year annuity-immediate paying \(10{,}000\) per year.
Since the first annuity payment occurs one year later, the value at age \(65\) is \(10{,}000a_{\overline{15}\,|0.05}\).
Set the accumulated deposits equal to the cost of the annuity: \(Xs_{\overline{25}\,|0.04}=10{,}000a_{\overline{15}\,|0.05}\).
generalization: Retirement funding problems often have two stages: an accumulation stage before retirement and a payout stage after retirement. First compute the fund needed at retirement using a present value annuity formula, then set it equal to the accumulated value of the deposits.
answer_text: The annual deposit is \(\boxed{2{,}492.36}\), so the correct choice is C.

title: Exercise 95
statement: At an effective annual interest rate \(i\), you are given: (i) the present value of an annuity-immediate with annual payments of \(1\) for \(n\) years is \(40\); (ii) the present value of an annuity-immediate with annual payments of \(1\) for \(3n\) years is \(70\). Calculate the accumulated value of an annuity-immediate with annual payments of \(1\) for \(2n\) years.
solution_steps: Split the \(3n\)-payment annuity into three blocks of \(n\) payments: \(a_{\overline{3n}\,|}=a_{\overline{n}\,|}+v^n a_{\overline{n}\,|}+v^{2n}a_{\overline{n}\,|}\).
The last \(2n\) payments, from time \(n+1\) to time \(3n\), have accumulated value \(X\) at time \(3n\).
The present value of this block is \(Xv^{3n}\).
Since the present value of the full \(3n\)-year annuity is \(70\), and the present value of the first \(n\)-year block is \(40\), write \(70=40+Xv^{3n}\).
generalization: When annuities are grouped into blocks of equal length, use identities such as \(a_{\overline{3n}\,|}=a_{\overline{n}\,|}+v^n a_{\overline{n}\,|}+v^{2n}a_{\overline{n}\,|}\). To find an accumulated value, identify the block whose accumulated value is being requested and discount it back to the comparison date.
answer_text: The accumulated value is \(\boxed{240}\), so the correct choice is C.

title: Exercise 96
statement: Harriet wishes to accumulate \(60{,}000\) in a fund at the end of \(25\) years. She plans to deposit \(80\) into the fund at the end of each of the first \(120\) months. She then plans to deposit \(80+X\) into the fund at the end of each of the last \(180\) months. Assume the fund earns interest at an annual effective rate of \(3.66\%\). Determine \(X\).
solution_steps: The deposits are monthly, so convert the annual effective interest rate to an effective monthly interest rate.
Let \(j\) be the effective monthly rate. Since the annual effective rate is \(3.66\%\), we have \(1+j=(1.0366)^{1/12}\).
The target date is the end of \(25\) years, which is the end of \(25\cdot12=300\) months.
View the deposits as \(80\) deposited every month for all \(300\) months, plus an extra \(X\) deposited every month for the last \(180\) months.
The accumulated value of the base deposits is \(80s_{\overline{300}\,|j}\).
The accumulated value of the extra deposits is \(Xs_{\overline{180}\,|j}\).
Thus, \(X\) is at least \(88\), but less than \(90\).
generalization: When deposits change after some time, one clean method is to split the cash flows into a base payment stream and an additional payment stream. Here, the payments can be viewed as \(80\) for all \(300\) months plus an extra \(X\) for the last \(180\) months.
answer_text: The value of \(X\) is \(\boxed{88.85}\), so the correct choice is C.

topic: annuities-immediate and accumulated value with changing interest rates
title: Exercise 97
statement: Jeff deposits \(100\) at the end of each year for \(13\) years into Fund \(X\). Rachel deposits \(100\) at the end of each year for \(13\) years into Fund \(Y\). Fund \(X\) earns an annual effective rate of \(15\%\) for the first \(5\) years and an annual effective rate of \(6\%\) thereafter. Fund \(Y\) earns an annual effective rate of \(i\). At the end of \(13\) years, the accumulated value of Fund \(X\) equals the accumulated value of Fund \(Y\). Calculate \(i\).
solution_steps: The key is to accumulate Fund \(X\) correctly because the interest rate changes after \(5\) years.
The first \(5\) deposits into Fund \(X\) accumulate at \(15\%\) until time \(5\), then at \(6\%\) from time \(5\) to time \(13\).
The accumulated value at time \(13\) of these first \(5\) deposits is \(100s_{\overline{5}\,|0.15}(1.06)^8\).
The last \(8\) deposits occur after the rate has changed to \(6\%\), so their accumulated value at time \(13\) is \(100s_{\overline{8}\,|0.06}\).
For Fund \(Y\), Rachel deposits \(100\) for \(13\) years at annual effective rate \(i\), so \(AV_Y=100s_{\overline{13}\,|i}\).
Since the accumulated values are equal, \(100s_{\overline{5}\,|0.15}(1.06)^8+100s_{\overline{8}\,|0.06}=100s_{\overline{13}\,|i}\).
generalization: When an interest rate changes during an accumulation period, split the deposits into groups based on when the rate changes. Accumulate each group using the appropriate rates, then equate the final accumulated values.
answer_text: The annual effective interest rate is approximately \(\boxed{7.4\%}\), so the correct choice is D.

title: Exercise 98
statement: Tom has served in the Navy for \(21\) years and plans to retire sometime in the future. Every three years, he has received a re-enlistment bonus and deposited a certain dollar amount, \(X\), in an account for when he retires. After making his current deposit, the value of his account is \(180{,}176\). Assuming an annual effective interest rate of \(9\%\), what were his tri-annual deposits?
solution_steps: The deposits are made every \(3\) years.
Since Tom has served for \(21\) years and has just made his current deposit, the deposits occur at times \(3,6,9,12,15,18,21\).
Since deposits are made every \(3\) years, use the effective interest rate per \(3\)-year period.
Let \(j\) be the effective rate per \(3\) years. Then \(1+j=(1.09)^3\).
The accumulated value after the current deposit is \(Xs_{\overline{7}\,|j}\).
generalization: When payments are made every \(k\) years, convert the annual effective interest rate into an effective rate per \(k\)-year payment period before applying the accumulated value formula.
answer_text: The tri-annual deposits were \(\boxed{10{,}405}\), so the correct choice is B.

title: Exercise 99
statement: At an annual effective interest rate of \(8\%\), the present value of a 30-year annuity-due with annual payments of \(X\) is \(1{,}500\). Determine \(X\).
solution_steps: Since this is an annuity-due, the payments occur at the beginning of each year.
Therefore, the payments occur at times \(0,1,2,\ldots,29\).
Using first principles, the present value is \(1{,}500=X+Xv+Xv^2+\cdots+Xv^{29}\).
Using a financial calculator, set BGN mode, \(N=30\), \(I/Y=8\), \(PV=-1{,}500\), and \(FV=0\).
generalization: For an annuity-due, payments occur at the beginning of each period. The present value formula is \(\ddot{a}_{\overline{n}\,|}=\dfrac{1-v^n}{d}\), and financial calculator problems should be done in BGN mode.
answer_text: The annual payment is \(\boxed{123.37}\), so the correct choice is C.

title: Exercise 100
statement: Given an annual effective discount rate of \(d=0.10\), calculate the present value of \(20\) payments of \(1{,}000\), paid every two years starting immediately.
solution_steps: Since payments are made every two years, we need the effective rate per two-year payment period.
The annual effective discount rate is \(d=0.10\).
Thus, the annual discount factor is \(v=1-d=0.90\).
The discount factor over two years is \(v^2=(0.90)^2=0.81\).
Therefore, the effective discount rate per two-year payment period is \(d_2=1-v^2=1-0.81=0.19\).
Since the payments start immediately, this is an annuity-due with \(20\) payments.
Using \(\ddot{a}_{\overline{20}\,|}=\dfrac{1-v^{20}}{d_2}\), where \(v=0.81\) is the two-year discount factor, we get \(PV=1{,}000\left(\dfrac{1-(0.81)^{20}}{0.19}\right)\).
generalization: When payments are made every \(k\) years, convert the given annual rate into an effective rate or discount factor per \(k\)-year payment period. For an annuity-due, use BGN mode on a financial calculator because payments occur at the beginning of each payment period.
answer_text: The present value is \(\boxed{5{,}185.36}\), so the correct choice is C.

topic: annuities-due and unknown monthly interest rate
title: Exercise 101
statement: A renter with \(1{,}104\) has a one-year lease. The landlord is willing to accept two payment options: (1) \(1{,}104\) now; or (2) \(100\) paid at the beginning of each month for \(12\) months. What monthly interest rate would be required for the two options to be equivalent?
solution_steps: The second option consists of payments made at the beginning of each month, so it is a monthly annuity-due.
Let \(j\) be the effective monthly interest rate.
The present value of the first option is \(1{,}104\).
The present value of the second option is \(100\ddot{a}_{\overline{12}\,|j}\).
Since the two options are equivalent, write \(1{,}104=100\ddot{a}_{\overline{12}\,|j}\).
Because the payments are made at the beginning of each month, use BGN mode on the financial calculator.
Since the calculator gives \(I/Y\) as a percentage, the monthly interest rate is \(j=1.55372\%=0.0155372\).
Thus, the required monthly interest rate is at least \(0.015\).
generalization: When payments are made at the beginning of each period, treat the stream as an annuity-due and use BGN mode on the financial calculator. If the calculator returns \(I/Y\), remember that it is usually expressed as a percentage.
answer_text: The required monthly interest rate is \(\boxed{0.0155372}\), so the correct choice is E.

title: Exercise 102
statement: The proceeds of a \(10{,}000\) death benefit are left on deposit with an insurance company for seven years at an annual effective interest rate of \(5\%\). The balance at the end of seven years is paid to the beneficiary in \(120\) equal monthly payments of \(X\), with the first payment made immediately. During the payout period, interest is credited at an annual effective interest rate of \(3\%\). Calculate \(X\).
solution_steps: Set up an equation of value at time \(7\).
The death benefit accumulates for \(7\) years at \(5\%\), so its value at time \(7\) is \(10{,}000(1.05)^7\).
At time \(7\), the beneficiary receives \(120\) monthly payments of \(X\), with the first payment made immediately.
Therefore, the payout stream is a monthly annuity-due.
During the payout period, the annual effective interest rate is \(3\%\).
Let \(j\) be the effective monthly interest rate. Then \(1+j=(1.03)^{1/12}\).
The value at time \(7\) of the payout stream is \(X\ddot{a}_{\overline{120}\,|j}\).
generalization: When a lump sum is accumulated first and then paid out as an annuity, choose the transition time as the equation-of-value date. If the payout begins immediately, use an annuity-due. If payments are monthly, convert the annual effective rate to an effective monthly rate before applying the annuity formula.
answer_text: The monthly payment is \(\boxed{135.27}\), so the correct choice is D.

title: Exercise 103
statement: Given \(d=0.04\), find the accumulated value at time \(20\) of an annuity with payments of \(250\) every two years. The first payment is made immediately and the last payment is made \(18\) years from now.
solution_steps: The payments are made every \(2\) years, starting immediately.
Thus, the payments occur at times \(0,2,4,\ldots,18\).
The accumulated value is requested at time \(20\), which is one two-year period after the last payment.
Therefore, this is an annuity-due accumulated value problem.
Since payments are made every \(2\) years, convert the annual effective discount rate into an effective interest rate per \(2\)-year period.
Therefore, the effective interest rate per \(2\)-year period is \(j=(0.96)^{-2}-1\).
Using the annuity-due accumulated value formula, \(AV=250\ddot{s}_{\overline{10}\,|j}\).
Using the TVM function, use BGN mode with \(N=10\), \(I/Y=100((1-0.04)^{-2}-1)\), \(PV=0\), and \(PMT=-250\).
generalization: When payments are made every \(k\) years, convert the annual rate into an effective rate per \(k\)-year payment period. If the first payment is made immediately and the accumulated value is measured one payment period after the last payment, use the annuity-due accumulated value formula \(\ddot{s}_{\overline{n}\,|}\).
answer_text: The accumulated value is \(\boxed{4{,}025.61}\), so the correct choice is C.

title: Exercise 104
statement: Bill deposits \(X\) into a savings account every six months. His first deposit is made today. The savings account earns a nominal annual interest rate convertible semiannually of \(i^{(2)}=0.06\). Bill's account balance immediately before his \(25\)th deposit is \(1{,}772.96\). Determine \(X\).
solution_steps: Deposits are made every six months, so there are two deposits per year.
The nominal annual interest rate convertible semiannually is \(i^{(2)}=0.06\).
Therefore, the effective interest rate per six-month period is \(j=\dfrac{0.06}{2}=0.03\).
Bill makes his first deposit today, so the deposit times are \(0,0.5,1,1.5,\ldots\).
The account balance is given immediately before the \(25\)th deposit, so the balance is measured at time \(12\), before the deposit at time \(12\) is made.
Thus, only the first \(24\) deposits are included.
These \(24\) deposits form an annuity-due accumulated one payment period after the last included deposit.
Using TVM, use BGN mode with \(N=24\), \(I/Y=3\), \(PV=0\), and \(FV=1{,}772.96\). Computing \(PMT\) gives \(-50\), so \(X=50\).
generalization: When the first deposit is made immediately, the deposits form an annuity-due. If the balance is given immediately before a future deposit, that future deposit is not included. Count only the deposits already made, then accumulate them to the valuation date.
answer_text: The deposit amount is \(\boxed{50}\), so the correct choice is C.

title: Exercise 105
statement: John wishes to accumulate \(15{,}000\) in a trust fund at the end of \(10\) years. He will make \(10\) annual deposits of \(1{,}000\), with each annual deposit made at the beginning of the year. In order to accumulate \(15{,}000\) in the trust fund, John needs to increase each of the last \(2\) payments by \(X\). The deposits earn interest at an effective annual rate of \(5\%\). Determine \(X\).
solution_steps: The deposits are made at the beginning of each year, so the \(10\) regular deposits of \(1{,}000\) occur at times \(0,1,2,\ldots,9\).
The target accumulated value is at time \(10\).
The last two deposits occur at times \(8\) and \(9\). These are the deposits increased by \(X\).
Split the deposits into two streams: \(1{,}000\) deposited at the beginning of each year for \(10\) years, and an additional \(X\) deposited at times \(8\) and \(9\).
The accumulated value at time \(10\) of the regular deposits is \(1{,}000\ddot{s}_{\overline{10}\,|0.05}\).
The accumulated value at time \(10\) of the two extra deposits is \(X\ddot{s}_{\overline{2}\,|0.05}\).
Therefore, the equation of value at time \(10\) is \(1{,}000\ddot{s}_{\overline{10}\,|0.05}+X\ddot{s}_{\overline{2}\,|0.05}=15{,}000\).
generalization: When only the last few payments are increased, split the cash flows into a base annuity and an additional annuity. Here, all \(10\) deposits of \(1{,}000\) form one annuity-due, while the extra \(X\) payments at times \(8\) and \(9\) form a 2-payment annuity-due accumulated to time \(10\).
answer_text: The required increase is \(\boxed{833.08}\), so the correct choice is A.

title: Exercise 106
statement: [SOA 2; SOA-IT.002] Kathryn deposits \(100\) into an account at the beginning of each \(4\)-year period for \(40\) years. The account credits interest at an annual effective interest rate of \(i\). The accumulated amount in the account at the end of \(40\) years is \(X\), which is \(5\) times the accumulated amount in the account at the end of \(20\) years. Calculate \(X\).
solution_steps: The deposits are made at the beginning of each \(4\)-year period, so they occur at times \(0,4,8,\ldots,36\).
Let \(j\) be the effective interest rate per \(4\)-year period. Then \(1+j=(1+i)^4\).
At time \(20\), there have been \(5\) deposits, at times \(0,4,8,12,16\).
Their accumulated value at time \(20\) is \(100\ddot{s}_{\overline{5}\,|j}\).
At time \(40\), there have been \(10\) deposits, so the accumulated value at time \(40\) is \(100\ddot{s}_{\overline{10}\,|j}\).
generalization: When deposits occur every \(k\) years, use an effective interest rate per \(k\)-year period. If deposits are made at the beginning of each period, use annuity-due accumulated value formulas. Ratios of accumulated values at different times can often be simplified by introducing a substitution such as \(y=(1+j)^m\).
answer_text: The accumulated value is \(\boxed{6{,}195}\), so the correct choice is E.

title: Exercise 107
statement: Given \(a_{\overline{8}\,|}=10\), find \(\ddot{a}_{\overline{9}\,|}\).
solution_steps: The symbol \(a_{\overline{8}\,|}\) represents the present value of \(8\) payments of \(1\), one period before the first payment.
Thus, \(a_{\overline{8}\,|}\) represents payments of \(1\) at times \(1,2,\ldots,8\).
The symbol \(\ddot{a}_{\overline{9}\,|}\) represents the present value of \(9\) payments of \(1\), measured at the time of the first payment.
Thus, \(\ddot{a}_{\overline{9}\,|}\) represents payments of \(1\) at times \(0,1,2,\ldots,8\).
Therefore, \(\ddot{a}_{\overline{9}\,|}\) equals the extra payment of \(1\) at time \(0\), plus the value of the payments at times \(1,2,\ldots,8\).
generalization: A useful identity is \(\ddot{a}_{\overline{n}\,|}=1+a_{\overline{n-1}\,|}\). This works because the annuity-due has one payment at time \(0\), followed by \(n-1\) payments that match an annuity-immediate.
answer_text: The value is \(\boxed{11}\), so the correct choice is C.

topic: annuities-immediate vs annuities-due and sinking funds
title: Exercise 108
statement: On June 1, 1970, a company begins making equal annual deposits into a fund to provide \(15{,}000\) a year for five successive years with which to retire a bond issue. The fund earns interest at an effective annual rate of \(3\%\). The last deposit into the fund is made on June 1, 1980. The first bonds are redeemed on June 1, 1980. Calculate the amount of the annual deposit.
solution_steps: The company makes deposits from June 1, 1970 through June 1, 1980.
Therefore, the deposits occur at times \(0,1,2,\ldots,10\).
The bond redemptions are \(15{,}000\) per year for five successive years, starting on June 1, 1980.
Therefore, the redemptions occur at times \(10,11,12,13,14\).
Use June 1, 1980 as the equation-of-value date.
The accumulated value of the deposits at June 1, 1980 is \(Xs_{\overline{11}\,|0.03}\).
The value at June 1, 1980 of the bond redemption payments is \(15{,}000\ddot{a}_{\overline{5}\,|0.03}\), because the first redemption occurs immediately.
Using TVM, first use BGN mode with \(N=5\), \(I/Y=3\), \(PMT=15{,}000\), and \(FV=0\) to compute the required fund at June 1, 1980: \(PV=-70{,}756.476\).
Then use END mode with \(N=11\), \(I/Y=3\), \(PV=0\), and \(FV=-70{,}756.476\) to compute \(PMT=5{,}524.49\).
generalization: When deposits and withdrawals occur on the same date, choose that date as the equation-of-value date. If withdrawals begin immediately, use an annuity-due present value for the withdrawal stream. If deposits are accumulated to the date of their last deposit, the accumulated value may match an annuity-immediate formula, even if the deposits began immediately.
answer_text: The annual deposit is \(\boxed{5{,}524.49}\), so the correct choice is C.

title: Exercise 109
statement: Which of the following does not accurately reflect the relationship between immediate and due, present and accumulated value annuities? 1. \(\ddot{a}_{\overline{n}\,|}=(1+i)a_{\overline{n}\,|}\). 2. \(\ddot{a}_{\overline{n}\,|}=1+a_{\overline{n-1}\,|}\). 3. \(\ddot{s}_{\overline{n}\,|}=(1+i)s_{\overline{n}\,|}\). 4. \(\ddot{s}_{\overline{n}\,|}=s_{\overline{n+1}\,|}-1\). 5. \(s_{\overline{n}\,|}=a_{\overline{n}\,|}(1+i)^{n-1}\).
solution_steps: The identity \(\ddot{a}_{\overline{n}\,|}=(1+i)a_{\overline{n}\,|}\) is correct because an annuity-due is shifted one period earlier than an annuity-immediate.
The identity \(\ddot{a}_{\overline{n}\,|}=1+a_{\overline{n-1}\,|}\) is correct because the annuity-due has one payment at time \(0\), followed by \(n-1\) payments at times \(1,2,\ldots,n-1\).
The identity \(\ddot{s}_{\overline{n}\,|}=(1+i)s_{\overline{n}\,|}\) is correct because the annuity-due accumulated value is one period after the corresponding annuity-immediate accumulated value.

The identity \(\ddot{s}_{\overline{n}\,|}=s_{\overline{n+1}\,|}-1\) is correct because \(s_{\overline{n+1}\,|}=1+(1+i)+\cdots+(1+i)^n\), while \(\ddot{s}_{\overline{n}\,|}=(1+i)+(1+i)^2+\cdots+(1+i)^n\).
The relationship between present value and accumulated value is \(s_{\overline{n}\,|}=a_{\overline{n}\,|}(1+i)^n\).
Therefore, statement 5 is incorrect because the exponent should be \(n\), not \(n-1\).
generalization: The correct connection between present value and accumulated value is obtained by moving the present value forward \(n\) periods: \(s_{\overline{n}\,|}=a_{\overline{n}\,|}(1+i)^n\). Be careful not to use \(n-1\), even though the first annuity-immediate payment is at time \(1\).
answer_text: The incorrect identity is statement \(\boxed{5}\), so the correct choice is E.

title: Exercise 110
statement: An annuity makes \(5\) payments of \(200\) every two years starting today. You are given \(\ddot{a}_{\overline{2}\,|}=1.943\) and \(\ddot{s}_{\overline{10}\,|}=13.972\). Find the accumulated value of the annuity two years after the last payment.
solution_steps: The annuity makes payments of \(200\) every two years starting today.
Therefore, the payments occur at times \(0,2,4,6,8\).
The last payment is at time \(8\), so two years after the last payment is time \(10\).
Use the fission method to replace each two-year payment of \(200\) by two annual payments of \(R\).
Thus, the original payment stream is equivalent to \(10\) annual payments of \(\dfrac{200}{\ddot{a}_{\overline{2}\,|}}\) made at times \(0,1,2,\ldots,9\).
The accumulated value at time \(10\) of these \(10\) annual payments is \(\left(\dfrac{200}{\ddot{a}_{\overline{2}\,|}}\right)\ddot{s}_{\overline{10}\,|}\).
generalization: The fission method replaces a less frequent payment stream by a more frequent equivalent payment stream. Here, each payment of \(200\) every two years is replaced by two annual payments whose annuity-due present value equals \(200\).
answer_text: The accumulated value is \(\boxed{1{,}438.19}\), so the correct choice is C.

title: Exercise 111
statement: Annuities A and B have the following payments. For end of years \(1\text{--}5\), Annuity A pays \(0\) and Annuity B pays \(K\). For end of years \(6\text{--}10\), Annuity A pays \(2\) and Annuity B pays \(0\). For end of years \(11\text{--}15\), Annuity A pays \(1\) and Annuity B pays \(K\). Annuities A and B have equal present values at an annual effective interest rate \(i\) such that \(v^5=\frac{1}{3}\). Calculate \(K\).
solution_steps: The present value of Annuity A is \(2a_{\overline{5}\,|}v^5+a_{\overline{5}\,|}v^{10}\).
The present value of Annuity B is \(Ka_{\overline{5}\,|}+Ka_{\overline{5}\,|}v^{10}\).
Since the present values are equal, \(2a_{\overline{5}\,|}v^5+a_{\overline{5}\,|}v^{10}=Ka_{\overline{5}\,|}+Ka_{\overline{5}\,|}v^{10}\).
generalization: When payments occur in equal-length blocks, value each block as an annuity-immediate and discount it to time \(0\). If the same annuity factor appears on both sides, it can often be canceled, leaving a simpler equation in powers of \(v\).
answer_text: The value is \(\boxed{K=0.7}\), so the correct choice is C.

topic: current value and varying interest rates
title: Exercise 112
statement: You are given that \(X\) is the current value at the end of year \(2\) of a 20-year annuity-due of \(1\) per annum, and the effective annual interest rate for year \(t\) is \(\frac{1}{8+t}\). Calculate \(X\).
solution_steps: A 20-year annuity-due has payments of \(1\) at times \(0,1,2,\ldots,19\).
The current value is requested at the end of year \(2\), so the valuation date is time \(2\).
The effective annual interest rate for year \(t\) is \(i_t=\frac{1}{8+t}\).
Therefore, the accumulation factor for year \(t\) is \(1+i_t=1+\frac{1}{8+t}=\frac{9+t}{8+t}\).
The payment at time \(0\) accumulates to time \(2\) through years \(1\) and \(2\): \(\left(1+\frac{1}{9}\right)\left(1+\frac{1}{10}\right)=\frac{10}{9}\cdot\frac{11}{10}=\frac{11}{9}\).
The payment at time \(1\) accumulates one year to time \(2\): \(1+\frac{1}{10}=\frac{11}{10}\).
The payment at time \(2\) is already at the valuation date, so its value is \(1=\frac{11}{11}\).
The payment at time \(3\) is discounted one year back to time \(2\): \(\left(1+\frac{1}{11}\right)^{-1}=\frac{11}{12}\).
The payment at time \(4\) is discounted two years back: \(\left(1+\frac{1}{11}\right)^{-1}\left(1+\frac{1}{12}\right)^{-1}=\frac{11}{12}\cdot\frac{12}{13}=\frac{11}{13}\).
Continuing this telescoping pattern, the current value is \(X=\frac{11}{9}+\frac{11}{10}+\frac{11}{11}+\frac{11}{12}+\cdots+\frac{11}{28}\).
generalization: With varying annual interest rates, value each payment directly at the chosen valuation date. Products of successive accumulation or discount factors may telescope, as they do here because \(1+\frac{1}{8+t}=\frac{9+t}{8+t}\).
answer_text: The current value is \(\boxed{\displaystyle \sum_{t=9}^{28}\frac{11}{t}}\), so the correct choice is B.

title: Exercise 113
statement: Mr. Smith wants to give his son an annuity of \(5{,}000\) per year starting on his \(21\)st birthday, which will be increased to \(10{,}000\) per year on his \(25\)th birthday, with the final payment on his \(30\)th birthday. What is the present value of that annuity on his son's \(10\)th birthday if the effective annual interest rate is \(5\%\)?
solution_steps: We value the annuity on the son's \(10\)th birthday.
The effective annual interest rate is \(i=0.05\), so \(v=\frac{1}{1.05}\).
The \(5{,}000\) payments at ages \(21\) through \(24\) form a \(4\)-payment annuity-immediate valued at age \(20\).
The value at age \(10\) of these payments is \(5{,}000a_{\overline{4}\,|0.05}v^{10}\).
The \(10{,}000\) payments at ages \(25\) through \(30\) form a \(6\)-payment annuity-immediate valued at age \(24\).
The value at age \(10\) of these payments is \(10{,}000a_{\overline{6}\,|0.05}v^{14}\).
Thus, the present value is at least \(36{,}500\), but less than \(37{,}000\).
Alternatively, the cash flows can be rewritten as \(10{,}000a_{\overline{20}\,|0.05}-5{,}000a_{\overline{14}\,|0.05}-5{,}000a_{\overline{10}\,|0.05}\), which gives the same result.
generalization: When payments change over time, split the stream into simpler deferred annuities or rewrite the cash flows as a large level annuity minus unwanted portions. The key is to value everything at the same date.
answer_text: The present value is \(\boxed{36{,}520.22}\), so the correct choice is D.

topic: deferred annuities and nominal interest rates
title: Exercise 114
statement: A loan of \(8{,}000\) is to be repaid in \(36\) equal monthly installments, with the first one paid six months after the loan is made. The nominal annual interest rate is \(10\%\) compounded quarterly. Determine the amount of the monthly payment.
solution_steps: Since the payments are monthly, first convert the nominal annual rate compounded quarterly into an effective monthly rate.
Let \(j\) be the effective monthly interest rate. Since one quarter contains \(3\) months, \((1+j)^3=1.025\).
The first payment is made \(6\) months after the loan is made, so the \(36\) monthly payments occur at months \(6,7,8,\ldots,41\).
A \(36\)-payment annuity-immediate with first payment at month \(6\) is naturally valued one month before the first payment, namely at month \(5\).
Therefore, the present value at month \(0\) is \(Xa_{\overline{36}\,|j}v_j^5\).
generalization: When payments are monthly but the interest rate is given as a nominal annual rate compounded quarterly, first convert to an effective monthly rate. If the first payment is delayed, value the annuity at its natural valuation date and then discount back to the loan date.
answer_text: The monthly payment is \(\boxed{268.66}\), so the correct choice is D.

title: Exercise 115
statement: Eloise plans to accumulate \(100{,}000\) at the end of \(42\) years. She makes deposits of \(X\) at the beginning of years \(1\) through \(14\), no deposits at the beginning of years \(15\) through \(32\), and deposits of \(Y\) at the beginning of years \(33\) through \(42\). The annual effective interest rate is \(7\%\). Also, \(X-Y=100\). Calculate \(Y\).
solution_steps: The deposits of \(X\) are made at the beginning of years \(1\) through \(14\), so they occur at times \(0,1,2,\ldots,13\).
The deposits of \(Y\) are made at the beginning of years \(33\) through \(42\), so they occur at times \(32,33,\ldots,41\).
The target accumulated value is at time \(42\).
The first set of deposits can be valued at time \(0\) using \(\ddot{a}_{\overline{14}\,|0.07}\), then accumulated to time \(42\).
Thus, the accumulated value at time \(42\) of the first set is \(X\ddot{a}_{\overline{14}\,|0.07}(1.07)^{42}\).
The last \(10\) deposits of \(Y\) form an annuity-due accumulated to time \(42\), so their value is \(Y\ddot{s}_{\overline{10}\,|0.07}\).
Set up the equation of value: \(X\ddot{a}_{\overline{14}\,|0.07}(1.07)^{42}+Y\ddot{s}_{\overline{10}\,|0.07}=100{,}000\).
Equivalently, the first set can be accumulated using \((Y+100)\ddot{s}_{\overline{14}\,|0.07}(1.07)^{28}\), which gives the same result.
generalization: When deposits occur at the beginning of years, treat them as annuities-due. Choose the final target date as the equation-of-value date, then accumulate each block of deposits to that date. If two unknown payments are related, substitute one in terms of the other before solving.
answer_text: The value is \(\boxed{479}\), so the correct choice is A.

title: Exercise 116
statement: Deposits of \(1{,}000\) are placed into a fund at the beginning of each year for \(30\) years. At the end of the \(40\)th year, annual withdrawals commence and continue forever. Interest is at an effective annual rate of \(5\%\). Calculate the annual withdrawal.
solution_steps: The deposits are made at the beginning of each year for \(30\) years, so they occur at times \(0,1,2,\ldots,29\).
The annual withdrawals start at the end of the \(40\)th year, so they occur at times \(40,41,42,\ldots\).
A convenient equation of value is at time \(39\), one year before the first withdrawal.
The deposits at times \(0,1,\ldots,29\) have accumulated value at time \(39\) equal to \(1{,}000s_{\overline{30}\,|0.05}(1.05)^{10}\).
The withdrawals at times \(40,41,42,\ldots\) form a perpetuity-immediate valued at time \(39\), so their value is \(Xa_{\overline{\infty}\,|0.05}\).
Equivalently, an equation at time \(40\) would use a perpetuity-due: \(1{,}000\ddot{a}_{\overline{30}\,|0.05}(1.05)^{40}=X\ddot{a}_{\overline{\infty}\,|0.05}\).
generalization: When deposits are made at the beginning of each year, they can be handled as an annuity-due or shifted into an annuity-immediate framework. For withdrawals that continue forever, choose the valuation date carefully: one period before the first withdrawal gives a perpetuity-immediate, while the date of the first withdrawal gives a perpetuity-due.
answer_text: The annual withdrawal is \(\boxed{5{,}411.09}\), so the correct choice is C.

title: Exercise 117
statement: A perpetuity of \(1\) each year, with the first payment due immediately, has a present value of \(25\) at an annual effective interest rate \(i\). The owner is considering exchanging it now for another perpetuity with the first payment due immediately and subsequent payments due at two-year intervals. What should the payment of the second perpetuity be in order to keep the same interest rate \(i\) and the same present value?
solution_steps: The first perpetuity pays \(1\) immediately and then \(1\) each year forever.
Thus, it is a perpetuity-due with annual payments of \(1\).
The second perpetuity pays \(X\) immediately and then every two years forever.
Let \(e\) be the effective discount rate per two-year period.
The second perpetuity is a perpetuity-due with payments every two years, so its present value is \(\frac{X}{e}\).
The present value must remain \(25\), so \(\frac{X}{e}=25\).
generalization: For a perpetuity-due, use the effective discount rate per payment period. If payments occur every two years, convert the annual discount factor to a two-year discount rate using \(e=1-v^2\).
answer_text: The payment of the second perpetuity is \(\boxed{1.96}\), so the correct choice is C.

title: Exercise 118
statement: John's estate is to be divided into three equal parts and invested, to be paid out as follows. John's two children will each receive their share in \(20\) level annual payments beginning one year after John's death. Charity Q will receive its share as equal annual payments in perpetuity beginning \(21\) years after John's death. Charity Q's annual payment is twice the annual payment for one child. Determine the effective annual interest rate at which the estate is invested.
solution_steps: The estate is divided into three equal parts, so the present value of each party's payments is equal.
Let \(X\) be the annual payment for one child.
Then Charity Q's annual payment is \(2X\).
Each child receives payments at times \(1,2,\ldots,20\).
Thus, the present value of one child's payments is \(Xa_{\overline{20}\,|}\).
Charity Q receives payments at times \(21,22,23,\ldots\).
This is a perpetuity-immediate beginning at time \(21\). Its value at time \(20\) is \(2Xa_{\overline{\infty}\,|}\).
Discounting back \(20\) years, Charity Q's present value is \(2Xa_{\overline{\infty}\,|}v^{20}\).
Since the present values are equal, \(Xa_{\overline{20}\,|}=2Xa_{\overline{\infty}\,|}v^{20}\).
generalization: When equal shares are paid through different annuity structures, equate the present values of the payment streams. A perpetuity-immediate beginning at time \(m+1\) has present value \(v^m a_{\overline{\infty}\,|}\) times the payment amount.
answer_text: The effective annual interest rate is \(\boxed{5.65\%}\), so the closest choice is C.

title: Exercise 119
statement: Perpetuity A pays \(100\) at the end of each year. Perpetuity B pays \(25\) at the end of each quarter. The present value of Perpetuity A at the annual effective interest rate \(i\) is \(2{,}000\). What is the present value of Perpetuity B at the same annual effective interest rate \(i\)?
solution_steps: Perpetuity A pays \(100\) at the end of each year, so it is a perpetuity-immediate.
The present value of Perpetuity A is \(100a_{\overline{\infty}\,|i}=2{,}000\).
So the annual effective interest rate is \(5\%\).
Perpetuity B pays quarterly, so we must convert the annual effective rate into an effective quarterly rate.
Let \(j\) be the effective quarterly interest rate. Then \((1+j)^4=1.05\).
Perpetuity B pays \(25\) at the end of each quarter, so it is a perpetuity-immediate with quarterly payments.
generalization: For a perpetuity, the interest rate must match the payment period. If payments are quarterly but the given rate is annual effective, first convert to the effective quarterly rate before using \(a_{\overline{\infty}\,|}=\frac{1}{i}\).
answer_text: The present value of Perpetuity B is \(\boxed{2{,}037.12}\), so the correct choice is E.

title: Exercise 120
statement: The death benefit on a life insurance policy can be paid in any of the following ways, each of which has the same present value as the death benefit: (i) a perpetuity of \(120\) at the end of each month; (ii) \(365.47\) at the end of each month for \(n\) years; and (iii) a payment of \(17{,}866.32\) at the end of \(n\) years. Calculate the amount of the death benefit.
solution_steps: Let \(j\) be the effective monthly interest rate.
Since all three payment options have the same present value, equate options (i) and (ii).
Option (i) is a monthly perpetuity-immediate of \(120\), so its present value is \(120a_{\overline{\infty}\,|j}\).
Option (ii) is a monthly annuity-immediate of \(365.47\) for \(n\) years. Since there are \(12n\) monthly payments, its present value is \(365.47a_{\overline{12n}\,|j}\).
Option (iii) gives the death benefit as the present value of \(17{,}866.32\) paid at the end of \(n\) years.
generalization: When several payment options have the same present value, equate two options to solve for a useful discount factor, then use that factor in the remaining option. Here, equating the perpetuity and finite annuity gives \(v_j^{12n}\), which is exactly the discount factor needed for the single payment option.
answer_text: The death benefit is \(\boxed{12{,}000}\), so the correct choice is D.

title: Exercise 121
statement: Deposits of \(1{,}000\) are placed into a fund at the beginning of each year for \(30\) years. At the end of the \(40\)th year, annual payments commence and continue forever. Interest is at an effective rate of \(5\%\). Calculate the annual payment.
solution_steps: The deposits are made at the beginning of each year for \(30\) years, so they occur at times \(0,1,2,\ldots,29\).
The annual payments begin at the end of the \(40\)th year, so they occur at times \(40,41,42,\ldots\).
A convenient equation of value is at time \(39\), one year before the first annual payment.
The accumulated value at time \(39\) of the deposits is \(1{,}000s_{\overline{30}\,|0.05}(1.05)^{10}\).
The payments beginning at time \(40\) form a perpetuity-immediate valued at time \(39\), so their value is \(Xa_{\overline{\infty}\,|0.05}\).
generalization: When deposits occur at the beginning of each year and withdrawals continue forever, choose a valuation date just before the first withdrawal. Then the withdrawals can be valued as a perpetuity-immediate, while the deposits can be accumulated to that same date.
answer_text: The annual payment is approximately \(\boxed{5{,}411.09}\), so the closest choice is D, \(5{,}410\).

title: Exercise 122
statement: Dave receives twenty payments of 500 every 6 months, with the first payment immediately. He immediately deposits each payment into an account earning an annual effective interest rate of i. Which of the following expressions represents the value of the account at time 10?
solution_steps: Payments are made every 6 months, so \(m=2\).
There are twenty semiannual payments, so the payments cover \(20/2=10\) years.
The first payment is made immediately, so the annuity is an annuity-due payable semiannually.
In \(m\)-thly annuity notation, the coefficient represents the total annual payment.
Each semiannual payment is \(500\), so the total annual payment is \(2(500)=1000\).
Therefore, the accumulated value at time \(10\) is \(1000\ddot{s}_{\overline{10}|i}^{(2)}\).
Equivalently, if \(j=(1+i)^{1/2}-1\) is the effective rate per 6 months, the value is \(500\ddot{s}_{\overline{20}|j}\).
generalization: For an annuity-due payable \(m\)-thly with payments of \(R\) each payment period for \(n\) years, the total annual payment is \(mR\), and the accumulated value at time \(n\) is \(mR\ddot{s}_{\overline{n}|i}^{(m)}\). Equivalently, using the effective rate \(j\) per payment period, the value is \(R\ddot{s}_{\overline{mn}|j}\).
answer_text: The correct answer is C. The value of the account at time 10 is \(\boxed{1000\ddot{s}_{\overline{10}|i}^{(2)}}\).

title: Exercise 123
statement: Given \(\ddot{a}_{\overline{3}|}^{(4)}=2.7732\) and \(v^3=0.8396\), what is the present value of 12 quarterly payments of 100, with the first payment one quarter from now?
solution_steps: There are 12 quarterly payments, so the payments cover \(12/4=3\) years.
Each quarterly payment is \(100\), so the total annual payment is \(4(100)=400\).
The present value of the quarterly annuity-immediate is \(PV=400a_{\overline{3}|}^{(4)}\).
We are given \(\ddot{a}_{\overline{3}|}^{(4)}\), which represents a quarterly annuity-due.
The annuity-due has payments at times \(0,0.25,\ldots,2.75\), while the annuity-immediate has payments at times \(0.25,0.50,\ldots,3\).
generalization: For an annuity-immediate payable \(m\)-thly over \(n\) years, each payment of \(R\) corresponds to a total annual payment of \(mR\). If the annuity-due factor is given instead, then \(a_{\overline{n}|}^{(m)}=\ddot{a}_{\overline{n}|}^{(m)}-\frac{1}{m}+\frac{1}{m}v^n\).
answer_text: The present value is \(\boxed{1093.24}\).

title: Exercise 124
statement: John made a deposit of 1000 into a fund at the beginning of each year for 20 years. At the end of 20 years, he began making semiannual withdrawals of 3000 at the beginning of each six months, with a smaller final withdrawal to exhaust the fund. The fund earned an annual effective interest rate of 8.16%. Calculate the amount of the final withdrawal.
solution_steps: The annual effective interest rate is \(i=0.0816\).
The deposits of \(1000\) are made at the beginning of each year for 20 years, so they occur at times \(0,1,2,\ldots,19\).
At time \(19.5\), the accumulated value of the deposits is \(1000s_{\overline{20}|0.0816}(1.0816)^{1/2}\).
The withdrawals begin at time \(20\). Relative to time \(19.5\), the first withdrawal occurs one half-year later, so the withdrawals form an annuity-immediate at the semiannual rate \(j=0.04\).
Let \(n\) be the number of full semiannual withdrawals of \(3000\) that would exhaust the fund. Then \(48444.38=3000a_{\overline{n}|0.04}\).
Therefore, there are 26 full withdrawals of \(3000\), followed by a smaller final withdrawal \(X\) six months later.
At time \(19.5\), the equation of value is \(48444.38=3000a_{\overline{26}|0.04}+X(1.04)^{-27}\).
generalization: When deposits and withdrawals have different payment frequencies, choose a valuation date that makes the cash flows easy to express. If the final withdrawal is smaller than the regular withdrawals, first determine how many full payments can be made, then solve an equation of value for the remaining partial payment.
answer_text: The final withdrawal is approximately \(\boxed{1430.35}\), so the correct answer is C.

title: Exercise 125
statement: Olga buys a five-year increasing annuity for X. Olga will receive 2 at the end of the first month, 4 at the end of the second month, and for each month thereafter the payment increases by 2. The nominal interest rate is 9% convertible quarterly. Calculate X.
solution_steps: The payments are monthly for 5 years, so the total number of payments is \(5\times 12=60\).
Factor out \(2\): the payment stream is \(2(1,2,3,\ldots,60)\).
Therefore, \(X=2(Ia)_{\overline{60}|j}\), where \(j\) is the effective monthly interest rate.
The nominal interest rate is \(9\%\) convertible quarterly, so the effective quarterly rate is \(0.09/4=0.0225\).
Using the TVM function in BGN mode with \(N=60\), \(I/Y=0.74444275\), \(PMT=1\), and \(FV=-60\), the calculator gives \(PV=-10.15873987\).
generalization: For monthly payments that increase arithmetically by a constant amount R, the payment stream \(R,2R,3R,\ldots,nR\) has present value \(R(Ia)_{\overline{n}|j}\), where \(j\) is the effective interest rate per payment period.
answer_text: The value of the annuity is \(\boxed{2729.21}\).

title: Exercise 126
statement: Smith makes deposits to a savings account on July 1 of each year. The first deposit was made on July 1, 1972, and the last deposit will be made on July 1, 1982. The first deposit was 1000, and each subsequent deposit increases by 100. The account is credited with an effective annual interest rate of 4%. Determine the value of Smith's account as of July 2, 1982.
solution_steps: Let time \(0\) be July 1, 1971. Then the deposits occur at times \(1,2,\ldots,11\).
The level part contributes \(900s_{\overline{11}|0.04}\) at time \(11\).
The increasing part has present value \(100(Ia)_{\overline{11}|0.04}\) at time \(0\).
Accumulating this increasing part to time \(11\) gives \(100(Ia)_{\overline{11}|0.04}(1.04)^{11}\).
generalization: When deposits increase arithmetically, it is often useful to decompose the payments into a level annuity plus an increasing annuity. For payments \(A,A+B,A+2B,\ldots,A+(n-1)B\), one can write the stream as \((A-B)(1,1,\ldots,1)+B(1,2,\ldots,n)\).
answer_text: The value of Smith's account is \(\boxed{19702.23}\).

title: Exercise 127
statement: Jones purchased a perpetuity today for 7000. He will receive the first annual payment of 200 five years from now. The second annual payment will be 200 plus an amount C. Each subsequent payment will be the prior amount plus an additional constant amount C. If the effective interest rate is 4%, find C.
solution_steps: The first payment is \(200\) at time \(5\). The second payment is \(200+C\) at time \(6\), the third payment is \(200+2C\) at time \(7\), and so on.
Decompose the payment stream into a level perpetuity of \(200\) beginning at time \(5\), plus an increasing perpetuity with payments \(C,2C,3C,\ldots\) beginning at time \(6\).
The level perpetuity has value \(200a_{\overline{\infty}|0.04}\) at time \(4\), so its present value is \(200a_{\overline{\infty}|0.04}v^4\).
The increasing part is an increasing perpetuity-immediate valued at time \(5\), so its value at time \(5\) is \(C(Ia)_{\overline{\infty}|0.04}\).
Therefore, the present value of the increasing part is \(C(Ia)_{\overline{\infty}|0.04}v^5\).
generalization: When a perpetuity has payments that start later and then increase arithmetically, decompose the stream into a level perpetuity plus an increasing perpetuity. Carefully choose the valuation date: a level perpetuity-immediate beginning at time \(t+1\) is valued at time \(t\), while an increasing perpetuity-immediate with payments \(1,2,3,\ldots\) beginning at time \(t+1\) is also valued at time \(t\).
answer_text: The constant increase is \(\boxed{5.10}\).

title: Exercise 127
statement: Jones purchased a perpetuity today for 7000. He will receive the first annual payment of 200 five years from now. The second annual payment will be 200 plus an amount C. Each subsequent payment will be the prior amount plus an additional constant amount C. If the effective annual interest rate is 4%, find C.
solution_steps: Decompose the cash flow into a level perpetuity of \(200\) starting at time \(5\), plus an increasing perpetuity with payments \(C,2C,3C,\ldots\) starting at time \(6\).
Write an equation of value at time \(4\).
The purchase price accumulated to time \(4\) is \(7000(1.04)^4\).
At time \(4\), the level perpetuity has value \(200a_{\overline{\infty}|0.04}\).
The increasing part starts with \(C\) at time \(6\), so at time \(4\) it has value \(C(Ia)_{\overline{\infty}|0.04}v\).
generalization: For a perpetuity with a level component and an arithmetic increasing component, decompose the payments into a level perpetuity plus an increasing perpetuity. Then choose a valuation date one period before the first level payment, so the level perpetuity can be written as \(Ra_{\overline{\infty}|i}\). If the increasing component starts one period later, discount its increasing perpetuity value by one additional factor \(v\).
answer_text: The value of \(C\) is approximately \(\boxed{5.10}\), so the correct answer is A: Less than \(6\).

title: Exercise 128
statement: Sally buys a house. She has limited initial funds, so she agrees to make 360 monthly payments as follows: (i) The first payment is to be 500, with each subsequent payment increasing by 10. (ii) The first payment is due one month after the date of the loan. (iii) The nominal annual interest rate is 6% compounded monthly. Determine how much Sally borrowed.
solution_steps: The nominal annual interest rate is \(6\%\) compounded monthly, so the effective monthly interest rate is \(j=0.06/12=0.005\).
The payments are \(500,510,520,\ldots\) for a total of \(360\) monthly payments.
Therefore, the amount borrowed is the present value \(PV=490a_{\overline{360}|0.005}+10(Ia)_{\overline{360}|0.005}\).
The correct answer is E, since the amount is at least \(290000\).
generalization: For an arithmetic increasing payment stream \(P,P+Q,P+2Q,\ldots,P+(n-1)Q\), it is often useful to decompose it as \((P-Q)(1,1,\ldots,1)+Q(1,2,3,\ldots,n)\). The present value is then \((P-Q)a_{\overline{n}|j}+Q(Ia)_{\overline{n}|j}\), where \(j\) is the effective rate per payment period.
answer_text: Sally borrowed approximately \(\boxed{297428.85}\), so the correct answer is E: At least \(\$290{,}000\).

title: Exercise 129
statement: Sally takes out a mortgage with the following repayment schedule: 100 at the beginning of each month in year 1, 200 at the beginning of each month in year 2, and in general 100n at the beginning of each month in year n, ending with 1500 at the beginning of each month in year 15. Sally will pay interest at an effective annual rate of 7%. Determine the present value of Sally's loan payments at the beginning of year 1, just before she makes her first 100 payment.
solution_steps: Let \(j\) be the effective monthly interest rate. Since the annual effective interest rate is \(7\%\), we have \(j=(1.07)^{1/12}-1\).
During year 1, Sally pays \(100\) at the beginning of each month, so the accumulated value at the end of year 1 is \(100\ddot{s}_{\overline{12}|j}\).
During year 2, Sally pays \(200\) at the beginning of each month, so the accumulated value at the end of year 2 is \(200\ddot{s}_{\overline{12}|j}\).
Continuing in the same way, year 15 gives an accumulated value of \(1500\ddot{s}_{\overline{12}|j}\) at the end of year 15.

After rolling up each year's monthly payments, the annual cash flows are \(100\ddot{s}_{\overline{12}|j},200\ddot{s}_{\overline{12}|j},\ldots,1500\ddot{s}_{\overline{12}|j}\) at times \(1,2,\ldots,15\).
This is an increasing annuity-immediate with annual effective interest rate \(7\%\).
The present value is approximately \(76637\), which falls in the interval from \(76500\) to \(77500\).
generalization: When payments are made monthly within each year but increase annually, it is often useful to first accumulate each year's monthly payments to the end of that year using a monthly annuity-due accumulated value factor. Then the resulting annual cash flows can be treated as an increasing annuity-immediate at the annual effective interest rate.
answer_text: The present value is approximately \(\boxed{76636.52}\), so the correct answer is C: At least \(\$76{,}500\), but less than \(\$77{,}500\).

title: Exercise 130
statement: A scholarship fund is to be established by making annual deposits as follows: at time 1, the deposit is 1000; at time 2, the deposit is 1000+500; at time 3, the deposit is 1000+500(2); in general, at time t, the deposit is 1000+500(t-1); and at time 10, the deposit is 1000+500(9). Beginning at time t=11, the fund will pay one scholarship of amount X annually into perpetuity. Assuming i=0.12, what is the largest value of X that the fund can provide?
solution_steps: Write an equation of value at time \(10\).
The level part accumulates to time \(10\) as \(1000s_{\overline{10}|0.12}\).
The increasing part starts after time \(1\), with payments \(500,1000,1500,\ldots,4500\) at times \(2,3,\ldots,10\). Its accumulated value at time \(10\) is \(500(Is)_{\overline{9}|0.12}\).
Beginning at time \(11\), the fund pays \(X\) annually forever. At time \(10\), this perpetuity has value \(Xa_{\overline{\infty}|0.12}\).
generalization: When deposits increase arithmetically and future payments are a perpetuity, choose the valuation date one period before the perpetuity begins. Accumulate the deposits to that date, decompose them into a level annuity plus an increasing annuity if useful, and equate the result to the present value of the perpetuity.
answer_text: The largest scholarship payment is approximately \(\boxed{5880.22}\), so the correct answer is B: At least \(\$5{,}500\), but less than \(\$6{,}000\).

title: Exercise 131
statement: Jane receives a ten-year increasing annuity-immediate paying 100 the first year and increasing by 100 each year thereafter. Mary receives a ten-year decreasing annuity-immediate paying X the first year and decreasing by X/10 each year thereafter. At an effective interest rate of 5%, both annuities have the same present value. Calculate X.
solution_steps: Jane's payments are \(100,200,300,\ldots,1000\). Therefore, Jane's present value is \(100(Ia)_{\overline{10}|0.05}\).
Mary's payments are \(X,9X/10,8X/10,\ldots,X/10\). This is equal to \((X/10)(10,9,8,\ldots,1)\).
Since the two present values are equal, \(100(Ia)_{\overline{10}|0.05}=(X/10)(Da)_{\overline{10}|0.05}\).
generalization: When two annuities have the same present value, write each cash flow in terms of standard arithmetic annuity factors. An increasing stream \(R,2R,\ldots,nR\) has present value \(R(Ia)_{\overline{n}|i}\), while a decreasing stream \(nR,(n-1)R,\ldots,R\) has present value \(R(Da)_{\overline{n}|i}\).
answer_text: The value of \(X\) is \(\boxed{864.12}\).

title: Exercise 132
statement: For a decreasing annuity-due with annual payments: (i) the first payment is 120, (ii) each subsequent payment decreases by 10, (iii) the last payment is 40, and (iv) the annual effective rate of interest is 5%. Find the accumulated value of the annuity payments one year after the last payment.
solution_steps: Since the payments decrease by \(10\), solve \(120-10(n-1)=40\). This gives \(n=9\).
Because this is an annuity-due, the payments occur at times \(0,1,2,\ldots,8\).
The accumulated value is required one year after the last payment, which is time \(9\).
Thus, the accumulated value at time \(9\) is \(AV=30\ddot{s}_{\overline{9}|0.05}+10(D\ddot{s})_{\overline{9}|0.05}\).
Alternatively, value the payments at time \(-1\): \(10(Da)_{\overline{9}|0.05}+30a_{\overline{9}|0.05}=591.67\).
Then accumulate from time \(-1\) to time \(9\): \(591.67(1.05)^{10}=963.77\).
generalization: For a decreasing annuity-due, it is often simpler to value the cash flows one period before the first payment using annuity-immediate formulas, then accumulate to the required date. A payment stream that does not end at the decrement amount can be decomposed into a level annuity plus a standard decreasing annuity.
answer_text: The accumulated value one year after the last payment is \(\boxed{963.77}\).

title: Exercise 133
statement: For an annuity-immediate with monthly payments: (i) the monthly payments for each year are level; (ii) the monthly payments for the first year are 100; (iii) each subsequent year the monthly payments decrease by 10, so year two has monthly payments of 90, year three has monthly payments of 80, and so on; (iv) there are ten years worth of payments; and (v) the annual effective interest rate is 8%. Find the present value of this annuity.
solution_steps: Let \(j\) be the effective monthly interest rate. Since the annual effective interest rate is \(8\%\), \(j=(1.08)^{1/12}-1\).
During year 1, the annuity pays \(100\) at the end of each month, so the accumulated value at the end of year 1 is \(100s_{\overline{12}|j}\).
During year 2, the monthly payment is \(90\), so the accumulated value at the end of year 2 is \(90s_{\overline{12}|j}\).
Continuing in the same way, the annual rolled-up cash flows are \(100s_{\overline{12}|j},90s_{\overline{12}|j},80s_{\overline{12}|j},\ldots,10s_{\overline{12}|j}\) at times \(1,2,3,\ldots,10\).
Factor out \(10s_{\overline{12}|j}\). The annual cash flows are \(10s_{\overline{12}|j}(10,9,8,\ldots,1)\).
generalization: When payments are monthly within each year but change annually, first roll up the monthly payments within each year to the end of that year using the effective monthly rate. The resulting annual cash flows can then be valued as an increasing or decreasing annuity at the annual effective interest rate.
answer_text: The present value of the annuity is \(\boxed{5113.31}\).

title: Exercise 134
statement: An annuity-immediate pays 10 at the ends of years 1 and 2, 9 at the ends of years 3 and 4, etc., with payments decreasing by 1 every second year, until nothing is paid. The effective annual rate of interest is 5%. Calculate the present value of this annuity-immediate.
solution_steps: Let \(j\) be the effective interest rate per two years. Then \(j=(1.05)^2-1=0.1025\).
The payments at the ends of years \(2,4,6,\ldots,20\) are \(10,9,8,\ldots,1\). Using two-year periods, their present value is \((Da)_{\overline{10}|0.1025}\).
The payments at the ends of years \(1,3,5,\ldots,19\) are also \(10,9,8,\ldots,1\). They occur one year earlier than the corresponding even-year payments.
Therefore, the present value of the odd-year payments is \(1.05(Da)_{\overline{10}|0.1025}\).
generalization: When a payment pattern changes every two years, it can be useful to group cash flows into two-year periods. If two identical decreasing streams occur one year apart, value one stream using the two-year effective rate, then adjust the other stream by one year of interest.
answer_text: The present value is approximately \(\boxed{78.41}\), so the correct answer is B: \(78\).

title: Exercise 135
statement: François purchases a 10-year annuity-immediate with annual payments of 10X. Jacques purchases a 10-year decreasing annuity-immediate which also makes annual payments. The payment at the end of year 1 is equal to 50. At the end of year 2, and at the end of each year through year 10, each subsequent payment is reduced from the previous year's payment by an amount equal to X. At an annual effective interest rate of 7.072%, both annuities have the same present value. Calculate X, where X<5.
solution_steps: François receives \(10X\) each year for 10 years, so his present value is \(10Xa_{\overline{10}|i}\).
Decompose Jacques's payments as a level annuity of \(50\), minus an increasing annuity of \(X\) starting one year after the first payment.
Since both annuities have the same present value, \(10Xa_{\overline{10}|i}=50a_{\overline{10}|i}-X(Ia)_{\overline{9}|i}v\).
Move the terms involving \(X\) to the left: \(10Xa_{\overline{10}|i}+X(Ia)_{\overline{9}|i}v=50a_{\overline{10}|i}\).
generalization: When a decreasing payment stream has the form \(A,A-X,A-2X,\ldots,A-(n-1)X\), it can be written as a level annuity of \(A\) minus an increasing annuity based on the reductions. If the reductions start in the second payment, the increasing annuity must be discounted by one period.
answer_text: The value of \(X\) is approximately \(\boxed{3.59}\), so the correct answer is D.

title: Exercise 136
statement: You are given an annuity-immediate paying 10 for 10 years, then decreasing by one per year for nine years, and paying one per year thereafter, forever. The annual effective rate of interest is 4%. Calculate the present value of this annuity.
solution_steps: The payment stream pays \(10\) for the first 10 years.
Then it pays \(9,8,7,\ldots,1\) for the next 9 years.
After that, it pays \(1\) per year forever.
Represent the annuity as the sum of three streams: a level annuity of \(10\) for 10 years, a deferred decreasing annuity for 9 years, and a deferred perpetuity of \(1\).
The present value of the first stream is \(10a_{\overline{10}|0.04}\).
The decreasing payments \(9,8,\ldots,1\) occur at times \(11,12,\ldots,19\). Their value at time \(10\) is \((Da)_{\overline{9}|0.04}\), so their present value is \(v^{10}(Da)_{\overline{9}|0.04}\).
The perpetuity of \(1\) begins at time \(20\). Its value at time \(19\) is \(a_{\overline{\infty}|0.04}\), so its present value is \(v^{19}a_{\overline{\infty}|0.04}\).
generalization: When an annuity has several phases, split the cash flow into separate annuity streams. Level payments can be valued using level annuity factors, decreasing payments using decreasing annuity factors, and payments continuing forever using perpetuity factors. Then discount each deferred stream back to time 0.
answer_text: The present value is approximately \(\boxed{119.40}\), so the correct answer is A: \(119\).
